title: Refresh Tokens Quiz
description: Test your knowledge about the critical role of refresh tokens in modern token-based security.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of a Refresh Token?
options: To encrypt user passwords.
To provide a second layer of authentication (MFA).
To request a new, fresh Access Token when the current one expires, without the user having to re-authenticate.
To increase the security of every single API request.

questions.question: How long should an Access Token typically last in a secure system?
options: Indefinite, until the user manualy logs out.
Very long (e.g., 30 days).
Short-lived (e.g., 15 minutes).
Exactly 24 hours.

questions.question: Where is the most secure place to store a Refresh Token on the client?
options: In the browser's localStorage.
In an httpOnly, secure, SameSite=Strict cookie.
In the browser's history.
Inside a hidden input field in the HTML.

questions.question: What is 'Refresh Token Rotation'?
options: When a user updates their password every month.
When the server issues a new refresh token every time a user requests a new access token and invalidates the old one.
When the server automatically logs out a user after a certain time.
When the server rotates its encryption keys.

questions.question: Why is it easier to revoke a Refresh Token than a purely stateless Access Token?
options: Because refresh tokens are usually stored in a database on the server.
Because they are longer-lived.
Because they only work on a specific device.
It's not, they are both impossible to revoke.
